{{Otheruses||The similarly named castle in [[Gifu Prefecture]]'s [[Ena City]]|{{ill|Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|ja|大井城 (美濃国)}}}} {{Infobox Japanese castle | name = Ōi Castle | pref = Nagano Prefecture | alias = Ishinarabi Castle, Ōjō, Kuroiwa Castle | structure = Hirayama-style castle | tower_struct = | builders = {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光}}? | build_year = [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏}}, a branch of the [[Seiwa Genji]] descending from {{ill|Ogasawara Nagachika|ja|小笠原長清}}. From its founding in [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺}} in [[Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行}} supported the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}, joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清}}, who were advancing toward [[Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}.
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸}}, the orphaned son of [[Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[Takeda Shingen]] in [[Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃}}, a retainer of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.
== References == {{Reflist}} {{Citation help}}
== Bibliography == * ''{{ill|Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|ja|日本城郭大系}}'' Vol. 8, Nagano-Yamanashi, 1980 * Masaharu Sasamoto, ''The Two Battles of Kawanakajima'', 1998 * Hisako Tanabe, ''Uesugi Norinaga'', 1999 * Yuki Sakata, ''Kantō Kubō Ashikaga Nariuji'', 2004 * Shinobu Ōi, ''Tales of the Ōi Clan'', 2005 * Toshio Ōi, ''Chronicles of the Iwao Family'' (Modern Japanese Translation), 1996
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www1.ocn.ne.jp/~oomi/sakugun.html|title=History of the Saku Area – Prosperity of Iwamurada|date=2015-07-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://rarememory.justhpbs.jp/ooi/ooi.htm|title=Rise and Fall of the Ōi Clan|date=2012-05-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ju8t-hnm/Shiro/TokaiKoshin/Nagano/Ooi/index.html|title=Ōi Castle: Buried Fortresses|date=2016-03-04}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏}}, a branch of the [[Seiwa Genji]] descending from {{ill|Ogasawara Nagachika|ja|小笠原長清}}. From its founding in [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺}} in [[Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行}} supported the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}, joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清}}, who were advancing toward [[Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}.
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸}}, the orphaned son of [[Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[Takeda Shingen]] in [[Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃}}, a retainer of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.

'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).

The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀}}.

{{Otheruses||The similarly named castle in [[Gifu Prefecture]]'s [[Ena City]]|{{ill|Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|ja|大井城 (美濃国)|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox Japanese castle | name = Ōi Castle | pref = Nagano Prefecture | alias = Ishinarabi Castle, Ōjō, Kuroiwa Castle | structure = Hirayama-style castle | tower_struct = | builders = {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=draft}}? | build_year = [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸|12=draft}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=draft}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=draft}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=draft}}, a branch of the [[Seiwa Genji]] descending from {{ill|Ogasawara Nagachika|ja|小笠原長清|12=draft}}. From its founding in [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=draft}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|12=draft}} in [[Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=draft}} supported the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏|12=draft}}, joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|12=draft}}, who were advancing toward [[Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=draft}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=draft}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀|12=draft}}.
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=draft}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|12=draft}}, the orphaned son of [[Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|12=draft}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|12=draft}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|12=draft}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|12=draft}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[Takeda Shingen]] in [[Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|12=draft}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|12=draft}}, a retainer of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.
== References == {{Reflist}} {{Citation help}}
== Bibliography == * ''{{ill|Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|ja|日本城郭大系|12=draft}}'' Vol. 8, Nagano-Yamanashi, 1980 * Masaharu Sasamoto, ''The Two Battles of Kawanakajima'', 1998 * Hisako Tanabe, ''Uesugi Norinaga'', 1999 * Yuki Sakata, ''Kantō Kubō Ashikaga Nariuji'', 2004 * Shinobu Ōi, ''Tales of the Ōi Clan'', 2005 * Toshio Ōi, ''Chronicles of the Iwao Family'' (Modern Japanese Translation), 1996
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www1.ocn.ne.jp/~oomi/sakugun.html|title=History of the Saku Area – Prosperity of Iwamurada|date=2015-07-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://rarememory.justhpbs.jp/ooi/ooi.htm|title=Rise and Fall of the Ōi Clan|date=2012-05-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ju8t-hnm/Shiro/TokaiKoshin/Nagano/Ooi/index.html|title=Ōi Castle: Buried Fortresses|date=2016-03-04}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses||The similarly named castle in [[Gifu Prefecture]]'s [[Ena City]]|{{ill|Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|ja|大井城 (美濃国)|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox Japanese castle | name = Ōi Castle | pref = Nagano Prefecture | alias = Ishinarabi Castle, Ōjō, Kuroiwa Castle | structure = Hirayama-style castle | tower_struct = | builders = {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=draft}}? | build_year = [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸|12=draft}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=draft}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=draft}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=draft}}, a branch of the [[Seiwa Genji]] descending from {{ill|Ogasawara Nagachika|ja|小笠原長清|12=draft}}. From its founding in [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=draft}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|12=draft}} in [[Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=draft}} supported the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏|12=draft}}, joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|12=draft}}, who were advancing toward [[Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=draft}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=draft}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀|12=draft}}.
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=draft}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|12=draft}}, the orphaned son of [[Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|12=draft}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|12=draft}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|12=draft}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|12=draft}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[Takeda Shingen]] in [[Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|12=draft}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|12=draft}}, a retainer of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.
== References == {{Reflist}} {{Citation help}}
== Bibliography == * ''{{ill|Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|ja|日本城郭大系|12=draft}}'' Vol. 8, Nagano-Yamanashi, 1980 * Masaharu Sasamoto, ''The Two Battles of Kawanakajima'', 1998 * Hisako Tanabe, ''Uesugi Norinaga'', 1999 * Yuki Sakata, ''Kantō Kubō Ashikaga Nariuji'', 2004 * Shinobu Ōi, ''Tales of the Ōi Clan'', 2005 * Toshio Ōi, ''Chronicles of the Iwao Family'' (Modern Japanese Translation), 1996
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www1.ocn.ne.jp/~oomi/sakugun.html|title=History of the Saku Area – Prosperity of Iwamurada|date=2015-07-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://rarememory.justhpbs.jp/ooi/ooi.htm|title=Rise and Fall of the Ōi Clan|date=2012-05-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ju8t-hnm/Shiro/TokaiKoshin/Nagano/Ooi/index.html|title=Ōi Castle: Buried Fortresses|date=2016-03-04}}

{{Otheruses||The similarly named castle in [[Gifu Prefecture]]'s [[Ena City]]|{{ill|Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|ja|大井城 (美濃国)|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox Japanese castle | name = Ōi Castle | pref = Nagano Prefecture | alias = Ishinarabi Castle, Ōjō, Kuroiwa Castle | structure = Hirayama-style castle | tower_struct = | builders = {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=draft}}? | build_year = [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸|12=draft}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=draft}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=draft}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=draft}}, a branch of the [[Seiwa Genji]] descending from {{ill|Ogasawara Nagachika|ja|小笠原長清|12=draft}}. From its founding in [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=draft}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|12=draft}} in [[Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=draft}} supported the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏|12=draft}}, joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|12=draft}}, who were advancing toward [[Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=draft}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=draft}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated {{ill|Ogasawara Nagahide|ja|小笠原長秀|12=draft}}.
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=draft}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|12=draft}}, the orphaned son of [[Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|12=draft}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|12=draft}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|12=draft}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|12=draft}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[Takeda Shingen]] in [[Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|12=draft}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|12=draft}}, a retainer of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.
== References == {{Reflist}} {{Citation help}}
== Bibliography == * ''{{ill|Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|ja|日本城郭大系|12=draft}}'' Vol. 8, Nagano-Yamanashi, 1980 * Masaharu Sasamoto, ''The Two Battles of Kawanakajima'', 1998 * Hisako Tanabe, ''Uesugi Norinaga'', 1999 * Yuki Sakata, ''Kantō Kubō Ashikaga Nariuji'', 2004 * Shinobu Ōi, ''Tales of the Ōi Clan'', 2005 * Toshio Ōi, ''Chronicles of the Iwao Family'' (Modern Japanese Translation), 1996
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www1.ocn.ne.jp/~oomi/sakugun.html|title=History of the Saku Area – Prosperity of Iwamurada|date=2015-07-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://rarememory.justhpbs.jp/ooi/ooi.htm|title=Rise and Fall of the Ōi Clan|date=2012-05-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ju8t-hnm/Shiro/TokaiKoshin/Nagano/Ooi/index.html|title=Ōi Castle: Buried Fortresses|date=2016-03-04}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses||[[岐阜県]][[恵那市]]にある同名の城|大井城 (美濃国)}} {{日本の城郭概要表 | name = 大井城 | pref = 長野県 | ar_called =石並城、王城、黒岩城 | struct = 平山城 | tower_struct = | builders = [[大井朝光]]? | build_y = [[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）頃 | rulers = [[大井光長]]、[[大井朝行]]、[[大井光栄|大井光長（光栄）]]、[[大井光矩]]、[[大井持光]]、[[大井安房丸]]など | reject_y = [[文明 (日本)|文明]]16年（[[1484年]]） | remains = 曲輪 | cultural asset = 長野県史跡<ref>{{Cite web|和書|date=2016-02-25 |url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf |title=県史跡 |format=PDF |publisher=長野県 |accessdate=2016-09-09 }}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | 地図=Japan Nagano | ラベル=大井城 | 緯度度=36|緯度分=16|緯度秒=29.8 | 経度度=138|経度分=28|経度秒=55.9 | アイコン=日本の城跡 | ラベル位置=bottom }} '''大井城'''（おおいじょう）は、[[長野県]][[佐久市]][[岩村田]]にあった[[日本の城]]。長野県指定[[史跡]]。[[信濃国|信濃]][[守護代]]も務めた[[大井氏]]の居城。別名は'''岩村田館'''。
== 歴史 == 大井城は佐久市岩村田の東北部に[[湯川 (北佐久郡)|湯川]]の断崖に沿って、北から石並城（いせならびじょう）、王城、黒岩城の三城からなり、[[清和源氏]][[小笠原長清]]の七男、清和源氏小笠原流[[大井氏]]の祖、[[大井朝光]]が[[大井荘 (信濃国)|大井荘]]地頭としてこの地に居を構えて（「[[四鄰譚藪]]」）以来、文明16年（1484年）に落城するまで、佐久郡東北部を中心に勢力を振るった大井宗家の本拠地であった。
[[弘安]]2年（[[1279年]]）、二代城主[[大井光長]]は創建した大井荘落合の[[新善光寺]]（佐久市鳴瀬）に、大檀那として梵鐘を寄進した。また、「[[一遍聖絵|一遍上人絵伝]]」に描かれた大井太郎（光長）の館が、どのあたりにあったのかは定かでない。
[[建武 (日本)|建武]]2年（[[1335年]]）[[中先代の乱]]が起こり、[[後醍醐天皇]]に反旗を翻した足利方（北朝）についた四代城主[[大井朝行]]（行時）は、東山道を鎌倉に向けて進む朝廷軍（南朝）一万余を佐久郡で迎え撃ち、[[忽那重清]]らと交戦したが（『於大井庄合戦』『忽那文書』等）、奮戦数日の後、ついに落城した。
その後、大井城は復旧し、五代城主[[大井光栄|大井光長（光栄）]]は信濃国守護代を務めた。その子の六代城主[[大井光矩]]は[[応永]]7年（[[1400年]]）の[[大塔合戦]]で守護[[小笠原長秀]]と国人衆（大文字一揆）の仲介をして、敗走する小笠原長秀を救った。
次の七代城主[[大井持光]]の代には大井氏全盛期となり、繁栄期の大井城下は「応永年中、大井越前守（持光）、関東管領[[足利持氏]]公に仕えて武功三軍に出たり、したがって大井の城も盛大なり。」「民家六千軒、交易四達し、賑い国府（松本市）にまされり」（「四鄰譚藪」）、[[真光寺]]、[[龍雲寺 (佐久市)|龍雲寺]]、その他の寺社が立ち並んでいた。持光時代の所領は六万貫といわれ、伴野氏、望月氏等の領地を除く北佐久郡のほとんどを領し、上州、武州にも所領を有し、京都参勤には1千騎を率いたといわれる。[[永享]]10年（1438年）に[[永享の乱]]が起こり、大井持光は足利持氏の遺児[[永寿王丸]]（後の古河公方[[足利成氏]]）を安原の[[安養寺 (佐久市)|安養寺]]に庇護した（「足利系図」）。
次の八代城主[[大井政光]]の代には文明4年（1472年）に甲斐に侵攻、文明9年（1477年）には甲斐勢が佐久に侵攻したが、これを打ち破っている。（「[[勝山記|妙法寺記]]」）
次の九代城主[[大井政朝]]は文明11年（1479年）に佐久郡の[[伴野氏]]との戦いに大敗し、大井政朝は生捕りとなり、大井氏執事[[相木昌朝|相木氏]]が討死する。弟の十代城主[[大井安房丸]]に代替わりした文明16年（1484年）、更級・埴科両郡から小県郡に勢力のあった[[村上政清]]が大軍を率いて佐久に攻め込み、大井城は焼け落ち岩村田城下は一挙に灰燼に帰した。城主大井安房丸は小諸に逃れ、大井朝光が大井城に居城してからおよそ二百六十余年、城は落ち岩村田大井宗家もここに滅亡した。
大井宗家は滅びたが、[[岩尾城 (信濃国)|岩尾]]、耳取、芦田、相木などの一門、家臣の所領は存続して、大井城主（岩村田城主）には長窪大井氏の[[大井玄慶]]（安房丸の子、政信）が継いだ。[[天文 (元号)|天文]]12年（1543年）、岩村田城主[[大井貞隆]]は甲斐の[[武田信玄]]に攻略され[[長窪城]]に追われた。[[天正]]10年（1582年）、武田氏が滅亡すると、岩村田城主[[大井雅楽助]]は[[徳川家康]]麾下の[[依田信蕃]]に下り、城を明け渡した。
大井城の遺構は[[元文]]年間（1736-1741年）に書かれた「四鄰譚藪」に記述があるが、現在はほとんど見るべきものが残っていない。主郭部は王城公園となり、県天然記念物の大ケヤキがある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|date=2016-02-25 |url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf |title=県天然記念物 |format=PDF |publisher=長野県 |accessdate=2016-09-09 }}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『[[日本城郭大系]]』 第8巻 長野・山梨 1980年 * 笹本正治『川中島合戦は二つあった』1998年 * 田辺久子『上杉憲実』1999年 * 坂田祐樹『関東公方成氏』2004年 * 大井信『大井氏ものがたり』2005年 * 大井敏夫『岩尾家譜（現代語訳）』1996年
== 外部リンク == *{{Wayback |url=http://www1.ocn.ne.jp/~oomi/sakugun.html |title=佐久地域の歴史～岩村田郷の繁栄の巻5 |date=20150702055515}} *{{Wayback|url=http://rarememory.justhpbs.jp/ooi/ooi.htm |title=佐久の大井氏盛衰記|date=20120502115036}} *{{Wayback|url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ju8t-hnm/Shiro/TokaiKoshin/Nagano/Ooi/index.html |title=大井城 ～埋もれた古城～ |date=20160304205507}} {{リンク切れ |date=2016-09 }} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20191110145833/http://utsu02.fc2web.com/shiro430.html 大井城～城と古戦場～]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:おおいしよう}} [[Category:長野県の城]] [[Category:信濃国の城]] [[Category:現存しない長野県の建築物]] [[Category:信濃大井氏|城おおい]] [[Category:長野県指定史跡]] [[Category:佐久市の建築物|廃おおいしよう]] [[Category:佐久市の歴史]]

次の七代城主[[大井持光]]の代には大井氏全盛期となり、繁栄期の大井城下は「応永年中、大井越前守（持光）、関東管領[[足利持氏]]公に仕えて武功三軍に出たり、したがって大井の城も盛大なり。」「民家六千軒、交易四達し、賑い国府（松本市）にまされり」（「四鄰譚藪」）、[[真光寺]]、[[龍雲寺 (佐久市)|龍雲寺]]、その他の寺社が立ち並んでいた。持光時代の所領は六万貫といわれ、伴野氏、望月氏等の領地を除く北佐久郡のほとんどを領し、上州、武州にも所領を有し、京都参勤には1千騎を率いたといわれる。[[永享]]10年（1438年）に[[永享の乱]]が起こり、大井持光は足利持氏の遺児[[永寿王丸]]（後の古河公方[[足利成氏]]）を安原の[[安養寺 (佐久市)|安養寺]]に庇護した（「足利系図」）。
次の八代城主[[大井政光]]の代には文明4年（1472年）に甲斐に侵攻、文明9年（1477年）には甲斐勢が佐久に侵攻したが、これを打ち破っている。（「[[勝山記|妙法寺記]]」）
次の九代城主[[大井政朝]]は文明11年（1479年）に佐久郡の[[伴野氏]]との戦いに大敗し、大井政朝は生捕りとなり、大井氏執事[[相木昌朝|相木氏]]が討死する。弟の十代城主[[大井安房丸]]に代替わりした文明16年（1484年）、更級・埴科両郡から小県郡に勢力のあった[[村上政清]]が大軍を率いて佐久に攻め込み、大井城は焼け落ち岩村田城下は一挙に灰燼に帰した。城主大井安房丸は小諸に逃れ、大井朝光が大井城に居城してからおよそ二百六十余年、城は落ち岩村田大井宗家もここに滅亡した。
大井宗家は滅びたが、[[岩尾城 (信濃国)|岩尾]]、耳取、芦田、相木などの一門、家臣の所領は存続して、大井城主（岩村田城主）には長窪大井氏の[[大井玄慶]]（安房丸の子、政信）が継いだ。[[天文 (元号)|天文]]12年（1543年）、岩村田城主[[大井貞隆]]は甲斐の[[武田信玄]]に攻略され[[長窪城]]に追われた。[[天正]]10年（1582年）、武田氏が滅亡すると、岩村田城主[[大井雅楽助]]は[[徳川家康]]麾下の[[依田信蕃]]に下り、城を明け渡した。
大井城の遺構は[[元文]]年間（1736-1741年）に書かれた「四鄰譚藪」に記述があるが、現在はほとんど見るべきものが残っていない。主郭部は王城公園となり、県天然記念物の大ケヤキがある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|date=2016-02-25 |url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf |title=県天然記念物 |format=PDF |publisher=長野県 |accessdate=2016-09-09 }}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『[[日本城郭大系]]』 第8巻 長野・山梨 1980年 * 笹本正治『川中島合戦は二つあった』1998年 * 田辺久子『上杉憲実』1999年 * 坂田祐樹『関東公方成氏』2004年 * 大井信『大井氏ものがたり』2005年 * 大井敏夫『岩尾家譜（現代語訳）』1996年
== 外部リンク == *{{Wayback |url=http://www1.ocn.ne.jp/~oomi/sakugun.html |title=佐久地域の歴史～岩村田郷の繁栄の巻5 |date=20150702055515}} *{{Wayback|url=http://rarememory.justhpbs.jp/ooi/ooi.htm |title=佐久の大井氏盛衰記|date=20120502115036}} *{{Wayback|url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ju8t-hnm/Shiro/TokaiKoshin/Nagano/Ooi/index.html |title=大井城 ～埋もれた古城～ |date=20160304205507}} {{リンク切れ |date=2016-09 }} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20191110145833/http://utsu02.fc2web.com/shiro430.html 大井城～城と古戦場～]

{{Otheruses||The similarly named castle in [[Gifu Prefecture]]'s [[Ena City]]|{{ill|Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|ja|大井城 (美濃国)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|qq=}}}} {{Infobox Japanese castle | name = Ōi Castle | pref = Nagano Prefecture | alias = Ishinarabi Castle, Ōjō, Kuroiwa Castle | structure = Hirayama-style castle | tower_struct = | builders = {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|qq=}}? | build_year = [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Awa-maru|qq=}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|qq=}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|qq=}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|qq=}}, a branch of the [[Seiwa Genji]] descending from [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagachika]]. From its founding in [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|qq=}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzenkō-ji|qq=}} in [[Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|qq=}} supported the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuna Shigekiyo|qq=}}, who were advancing toward [[Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|qq=}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|qq=}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]].
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|qq=}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eijū ōmaru|qq=}}, the orphaned son of [[Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'yō-ji|qq=}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Masakiyo|qq=}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwao clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aiki clan|qq=}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Genkei|qq=}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[Takeda Shingen]] in [[Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Gakujo|qq=}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoda Nobushige|qq=}}, a retainer of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.
== References == {{Reflist}} {{Citation help}}
== Bibliography == * ''{{ill|Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|ja|日本城郭大系|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|qq=}}'' Vol. 8, Nagano-Yamanashi, 1980 * Masaharu Sasamoto, ''The Two Battles of Kawanakajima'', 1998 * Hisako Tanabe, ''Uesugi Norinaga'', 1999 * Yuki Sakata, ''Kantō Kubō Ashikaga Nariuji'', 2004 * Shinobu Ōi, ''Tales of the Ōi Clan'', 2005 * Toshio Ōi, ''Chronicles of the Iwao Family'' (Modern Japanese Translation), 1996
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www1.ocn.ne.jp/~oomi/sakugun.html|title=History of the Saku Area – Prosperity of Iwamurada|date=2015-07-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://rarememory.justhpbs.jp/ooi/ooi.htm|title=Rise and Fall of the Ōi Clan|date=2012-05-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ju8t-hnm/Shiro/TokaiKoshin/Nagano/Ooi/index.html|title=Ōi Castle: Buried Fortresses|date=2016-03-04}}

In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Masakiyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwao clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aiki clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Genkei|qq=}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[Takeda Shingen]] in [[Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Gakujo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoda Nobushige|qq=}}, a retainer of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.

{{Otheruses||The similarly named castle in [[Gifu Prefecture]]'s [[Ena City]]|{{ill|Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|ja|大井城 (美濃国)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|qq=}}}} {{Infobox Japanese castle | name = Ōi Castle | pref = Nagano Prefecture | alias = Ishinarabi Castle, Ōjō, Kuroiwa Castle | structure = Hirayama-style castle | tower_struct = | builders = {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|qq=}}? | build_year = [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Awa-maru|qq=}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|qq=}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the [[Seiwa Genji]] descending from [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagachika]]. From its founding in [[Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|qq=}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzenkō-ji|qq=}} in [[Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|qq=}} supported the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuna Shigekiyo|qq=}}, who were advancing toward [[Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|qq=}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|qq=}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]].
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|qq=}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eijū ōmaru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the orphaned son of [[Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'yō-ji|qq=}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Masakiyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwao clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aiki clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Genkei|qq=}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[Takeda Shingen]] in [[Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Gakujo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoda Nobushige|qq=}}, a retainer of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.

| build_year = [[:en:Jōkyū|Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|qq=|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432140}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|qq=|1=Ōi Tomoyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432189}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|qq=|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432137}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|qq=|1=Ōi Mitsunori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432141}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|qq=|1=Ōi Mochimitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220996}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Awa-maru|qq=|1=Ōi Awamaru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22124423}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[:en:Historic Site|Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|qq=|1=Ōi Tomomitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432187}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] descending from [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagachika]]. From its founding in [[:en:Jōkyū|Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|qq=|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432140}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzenkō-ji|qq=|1=Shinzenkō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501510}} in [[:en:Naruse|Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[:en:Kemmu Restoration|Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|qq=|1=Ōi Tomoyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432189}} supported the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuna Shigekiyo|qq=|1=Kutsuna Shigekiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56253681}}, who were advancing toward [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|qq=|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432137}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|qq=|1=Ōi Mitsunori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432141}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]].
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|qq=|1=Ōi Mochimitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220996}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[:en:Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[:en:Eikyō|Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eijū ōmaru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the orphaned son of [[:en:Ashikaga Mochiuji|Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'yō-ji|qq=|1=An'yō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043045}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Masakiyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwao clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aiki clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Genkei|qq=|1=Ōi Haruyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920475}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] in [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Gakujo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoda Nobushige|qq=|1=Yoda Nobushige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385519}}, a retainer of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[:en:Zelkova|Zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.
== References == {{Reflist}} {{Citation help}}
== Bibliography == * ''{{ill|Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|ja|日本城郭大系|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|qq=|1=Japanese Castle Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507767}}'' Vol. 8, Nagano-Yamanashi, 1980 * Masaharu Sasamoto, ''The Two Battles of Kawanakajima'', 1998 * Hisako Tanabe, ''Uesugi Norinaga'', 1999 * Yuki Sakata, ''Kantō Kubō Ashikaga Nariuji'', 2004 * Shinobu Ōi, ''Tales of the Ōi Clan'', 2005 * Toshio Ōi, ''Chronicles of the Iwao Family'' (Modern Japanese Translation), 1996
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www1.ocn.ne.jp/~oomi/sakugun.html|title=History of the Saku Area – Prosperity of Iwamurada|date=2015-07-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://rarememory.justhpbs.jp/ooi/ooi.htm|title=Rise and Fall of the Ōi Clan|date=2012-05-02}} * {{Wayback|url=http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ju8t-hnm/Shiro/TokaiKoshin/Nagano/Ooi/index.html|title=Ōi Castle: Buried Fortresses|date=2016-03-04}}

Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[:en:Zelkova|Zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大井城]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11432148]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses||The similarly named castle in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]]'s [[:en:Ena City|Ena City]]|{{ill|Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|ja|大井城 (美濃国)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|1=Oi Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22118066}}}} {{Infobox Japanese castle | name = Ōi Castle | pref = Nagano Prefecture | alias = Ishinarabi Castle, Ōjō, Kuroiwa Castle | structure = Hirayama-style castle | tower_struct = | builders = {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|1=Ōi Tomomitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432187}}? | build_year = [[:en:Jōkyū|Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432140}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|1=Ōi Tomoyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432189}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432137}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|1=Ōi Mitsunori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432141}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|1=Ōi Mochimitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220996}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Awa-maru|1=Ōi Awamaru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22124423}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[:en:Historic Site|Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|1=Ōi Tomomitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432187}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] descending from [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagachika]]. From its founding in [[:en:Jōkyū|Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432140}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzenkō-ji|1=Shinzenkō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501510}} in [[:en:Naruse|Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[:en:Kemmu Restoration|Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|1=Ōi Tomoyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432189}} supported the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuna Shigekiyo|1=Kutsuna Shigekiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56253681}}, who were advancing toward [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432137}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|1=Ōi Mitsunori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432141}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]].
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|1=Ōi Mochimitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220996}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[:en:Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[:en:Eikyō|Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eijū ōmaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the orphaned son of [[:en:Ashikaga Mochiuji|Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'yō-ji|1=An'yō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043045}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Masakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwao clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aiki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Genkei|1=Ōi Haruyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920475}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] in [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Gakujo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoda Nobushige|1=Yoda Nobushige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385519}}, a retainer of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[:en:Zelkova|Zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大井城]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11432148]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses||The similarly named castle in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]]'s [[:en:Ena City|Ena City]]|{{ill|Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|ja|大井城 (美濃国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Castle (Mino Province)|1=Oi Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22118066}}}} {{Infobox Japanese castle | name = Ōi Castle | pref = Nagano Prefecture | alias = Ishinarabi Castle, Ōjō, Kuroiwa Castle | structure = Hirayama-style castle | tower_struct = | builders = {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|1=Ōi Tomomitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432187}}? | build_year = [[:en:Jōkyū|Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) | rulers = {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432140}}, {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|1=Ōi Tomoyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432189}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432137}}, {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|1=Ōi Mitsunori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432141}}, {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|1=Ōi Mochimitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220996}}, {{ill|Ōi Awa-maru|ja|大井安房丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Awa-maru|1=Ōi Awamaru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22124423}}, among others | destroyed_year = [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484) | remains = Kuruwa (enclosures) | cultural_asset = Nagano Prefecture Historic Site<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/13ken-siseki.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref> | location=|latd=36|latm=16|lats=29.8 | longd=138|longm=28|longs=55.9 | map=Japan Nagano | label=Ōi Castle | latitude_deg=36|latitude_min=16|latitude_sec=29.8 | longitude_deg=138|longitude_min=28|longitude_sec=55.9 | icon=Japanese castle ruins | label_position=bottom }}
'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[:en:Historic Site|Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|1=Ōi Tomomitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432187}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] descending from [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagachika]]. From its founding in [[:en:Jōkyū|Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432140}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzenkō-ji|1=Shinzenkō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501510}} in [[:en:Naruse|Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[:en:Kemmu Restoration|Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|1=Ōi Tomoyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432189}} supported the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuna Shigekiyo|1=Kutsuna Shigekiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56253681}}, who were advancing toward [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432137}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|1=Ōi Mitsunori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432141}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]].
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|1=Ōi Mochimitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220996}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[:en:Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[:en:Eikyō|Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eijū ōmaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the orphaned son of [[:en:Ashikaga Mochiuji|Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'yō-ji|1=An'yō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043045}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Masakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.
Though the main line perished, branches like the {{ill|Iwao clan|ja|岩尾氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwao clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Aiki clan|ja|相木氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aiki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} persisted. {{ill|Ōi Genkei|ja|大井玄慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Genkei|1=Ōi Haruyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920475}} inherited the castle but was ousted by [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] in [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 12 (1543). Later, during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Gakujo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoda Nobushige|1=Yoda Nobushige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385519}}, a retainer of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[:en:Zelkova|Zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.

'''Ōi Castle''' (大井城, ''Ōi-jō'') was a [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]] located in [[Iwamurada]], [[Saku City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is a Nagano Prefecture-designated [[:en:Historic Site|Historic Site]] and was the seat of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who served as deputies to the [[Governor of Shinano Province]]. The castle was also known as '''Iwamurada Residence''' (岩村田館).
== History ==
Ōi Castle was located in northeastern Saku City, along the [[Yukawa River (Nagano)|Yukawa]]'s cliffs. It consisted of three fortifications: Ishinarabi Castle (石並城), Ōjō (王城), and Kuroiwa Castle (黒岩城). According to the ''[[Shirin Danzō]]'', the castle was established by {{ill|Ōi Tomomitsu|ja|大井朝光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Tomomitsu|1=Ōi Tomomitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432187}}, the progenitor of the {{ill|Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja|信濃大井氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan (Shinano Province)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] descending from [[:en:Ogasawara Nagakiyo|Ogasawara Nagachika]]. From its founding in [[:en:Jōkyū|Jōkyū]] 3 (1221) until its fall in [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), the Ōi clan controlled northeastern Saku District from this base.
In [[Kōan (era)|Kōan]] 2 (1279), the second lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunaga|ja|大井光長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunaga|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432140}} donated a temple bell to the {{ill|Shinzenkō-ji|ja|新善光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzenkō-ji|1=Shinzenkō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501510}} in [[:en:Naruse|Naruse]], Saku. The exact location of the manor depicted in the ''Illustrated Biography of Ippen'' remains uncertain.
During the [[:en:Kemmu Restoration|Kemmu Restoration]] in [[Kemmu (era)|Kemmu]] 2 (1335), the fourth lord {{ill|Ōi Chikayuki|ja|大井朝行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Chikayuki|1=Ōi Tomoyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432189}} supported the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], joining the Northern Court. He clashed with the Southern Court forces, including {{ill|Kutsuna Shigekiyo|ja|忽那重清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuna Shigekiyo|1=Kutsuna Shigekiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56253681}}, who were advancing toward [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] along the Eastern Mountain Road. Despite several days of fierce resistance, the castle eventually fell, as recorded in documents like ''Battle of Ōi Manor'' and ''Kutsuna Papers''.
The castle was later rebuilt. The fifth lord, {{ill|Ōi Mitsusaka|ja|大井光栄|lt=Ōi Mitsunaga (Mitsusaka)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsusaka|1=Ōi Mitsunaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432137}}, served as Deputy Governor of Shinano Province. His son, the sixth lord {{ill|Ōi Mitsunori|ja|大井光矩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mitsunori|1=Ōi Mitsunori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432141}}, mediated during the [[Ōtō Rebellion]] in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 7 (1400), aiding the defeated [[:en:Ogasawara Nagahide|Ogasawara Nagahide]].
Under the seventh lord {{ill|Ōi Mochimitsu|ja|大井持光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Mochimitsu|1=Ōi Mochimitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220996}}, the clan reached its zenith. The Ōi castle town flourished, with over 6,000 households, surpassing the provincial capital ([[:en:Matsumoto City|Matsumoto]]). Mochimitsu's reign saw vast territorial control, including 60,000 ''koku'' in northern Saku and estates in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]]. He reportedly led 1,000 troops to Kyoto.
In [[:en:Eikyō|Eikyō]] 10 (1438), Mochimitsu sheltered {{ill|Eijū ōmaru|ja|永寿王丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eijū ōmaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the orphaned son of [[:en:Ashikaga Mochiuji|Ashikaga Mochiuji]], at {{ill|An'yō-ji|ja|安養寺 (佐久市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'yō-ji|1=An'yō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043045}} during the [[Eikyō Rebellion]].
Subsequent lords faced difficulties. In [[Bunmei (era)|Bunmei]] 16 (1484), {{ill|Murakami Masakiyo|ja|村上政清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Masakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} attacked Ōi Castle, which was set ablaze, marking the end of the Ōi main family’s 260-year rule.

Later, during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, {{ill|Ōi Gakujo|ja|大井雅楽助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Gakujo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} surrendered the castle to {{ill|Yoda Nobushige|ja|依田信蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoda Nobushige|1=Yoda Nobushige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11385519}}, a retainer of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]].
Today, little remains of the castle. The main enclosure is a public park, home to a giant [[:en:Zelkova|Zelkova]] designated a prefectural natural monument<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Natural Monuments|url=http://www.pref.nagano.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunsho/bunka/rekishi/documents/15ken-tennenkinenbutu.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Nagano Prefecture|date=2016-02-25|accessdate=2016-09-09}}</ref>.
